title: Protecting Access for Hunters and Anglers Act
short_title: Protecting Access for Hunters and Anglers Act
plain_summary: This bill stops three federal agencies from banning lead ammunition and fishing tackle on federal lands where hunting and fishing are allowed. The agencies that cannot make these bans are the Fish and Wildlife Service, the Bureau of Land Management, and the Forest Service. There are only two exceptions to this rule. First, any lead bans that already exist can stay in place. Second, these agencies can ban lead in a specific area only if they have proof from that exact location showing that lead is the main reason wildlife populations are dropping, and only if the state government agrees with the ban.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Protecting Access for Hunters and Anglers Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Protecting Access for Hunters and Anglers on Federal Land and Water
sections.text: In general, the Secretary of the Interior and the Secretary of Agriculture cannot ban hunters and anglers from using lead bullets or fishing gear on federal lands and waters. This includes lands managed by the Fish and Wildlife Service, Bureau of Land Management, and Forest Service. These government officials also cannot make rules about how much lead can be in bullets or fishing gear used on these federal areas that are open for hunting and fishing. However, there is an exception to this rule. The government can ban lead bullets or fishing gear in a specific area if two things are true. First, they must have scientific data from that exact area showing that lead is the main cause of wildlife dying there. Second, the ban must follow the laws of the state where the federal land is located, match the state fish and wildlife department policies, or be approved by that state department. When the government makes such a ban, they must publish a notice in the Federal Register explaining how their ban meets these requirements.
